name: Coconut
crop_cycle_note: Perennial palm; first bearing in 5-7 years (dwarf/hybrid) to 6-10 years (tall varieties), productive for 60+ years

growth_stages.name: Nursery / Seedling
care_focus: Polybag or nursery bed with well-drained sandy loam

growth_stages.name: Juvenile / Vegetative
characteristics: Rapid leaf production, trunk not yet visible or just starting to thicken
care_focus: Regular irrigation especially in dry months
Watch for leaf-eating caterpillars and rhinoceros beetle damage on young leaves

growth_stages.name: Pre-Flowering
characteristics: Crown broadens, leaf scars visible on trunk base
care_focus: Increase potassium and boron application to support flowering
Monitor crown region closely for early bud rot signs

growth_stages.name: Flowering / Button Stage
characteristics: Spadix opens, male and female flowers visible
Small buttons (pin-head nuts) form after pollination
care_focus: Avoid moisture stress - button shedding risk is high
Manual pollination assistance in low-yield palms if needed

growth_stages.name: Nut Development
characteristics: Nuts enlarging, husk still green and soft
Water content high, kernel not yet formed
care_focus: Sustain irrigation - water stress now causes immature nut fall
Protect bunches from rodent and squirrel damage

growth_stages.name: Maturity / Harvest
characteristics: Husk turns brown, nut sounds hollow when tapped
Kernel fully formed and firm, copra-ready
care_focus: Harvest every 45-60 days to avoid over-maturity and nut fall losses
Post-harvest: dry husks and store copra away from moisture

operations: Basin irrigation twice a week in absence of rain
Mulching with coconut husk/coir to conserve soil moisture
Apply organic manure/compost before monsoon onset
precautions: Watch for red palm weevil activity, most active in hot dry weather
Avoid basin flooding that stresses roots before rain arrives

operations: Ensure drainage channels are clear to prevent waterlogging
Apply first split of fertilizer dose after soil is moist
Inspect crown region for bud rot after heavy continuous rain
precautions: High risk period for bud rot (Phytophthora) and stem bleeding disease
Avoid basal application of fresh manure that can encourage fungal growth in waterlogged soil

operations: Deweeding and basin opening for aeration
Check for rhinoceros beetle damage on unopened fronds
precautions: Residual soil moisture can still favor fungal disease - keep monitoring crown

operations: Reduce irrigation frequency slightly, monitor soil moisture
Apply potash and micronutrients (boron) for flowering support
precautions: Cooler nights can slow nut development - avoid additional stress from irrigation neglect

diseases.name: Bud Rot
symptoms: Foul-smelling rot at the spindle/crown region
Youngest leaves yellow, wilt, and can be pulled out easily
Crown eventually collapses if untreated
treatment: Remove and destroy all rotten tissue from the crown immediately
Apply Bordeaux paste or copper oxychloride paste to the cleaned wound
Drench crown region with a copper-based fungicide solution
prevention: Improve drainage around the palm base
Avoid mechanical injury to the crown during pruning
Prophylactic copper spray before monsoon onset in endemic areas

diseases.name: Stem Bleeding Disease
symptoms: Reddish-brown liquid oozing from cracks in the trunk
Internal tissue turns brown and friable near the bleeding patch
Gradual decline in nut yield
treatment: Chisel out the affected tissue and apply Bordeaux paste to the wound
Apply a fungicide (e.g. tridemorph) drench near the affected area
Improve overall palm nutrition to aid recovery
prevention: Avoid trunk injuries from tools or animals
Ensure balanced potassium nutrition, deficiency worsens severity

diseases.name: Grey Leaf Blight
symptoms: Small yellowish spots on leaflets that enlarge into grey-brown lesions with yellow halo
Lesions coalesce causing leaf tip drying
Mostly affects nutrient-stressed or older palms
treatment: Remove and burn severely affected leaves
Spray with mancozeb or copper oxychloride at 0.2% concentration
Correct nutrient deficiencies, especially nitrogen and potassium
prevention: Avoid overcrowded planting that limits air circulation

diseases.name: Root (Wilt) Disease
symptoms: Ribboning of leaflets and marginal yellowing starting from older leaves
Reduction in canopy size and nut yield over years
Roots show progressive decay, few functional roots remain
treatment: No direct cure; apply integrated nutrient management to slow decline
Apply potassium, magnesium, and micronutrient mixtures as supportive care
Remove severely declined, unproductive palms to reduce disease spread
prevention: Plant disease-tolerant hybrid varieties in endemic tracts
Maintain good drainage and organic matter in soil

diseases.name: Premature Nut Fall / Button Shedding
symptoms: Buttons and immature nuts fall before maturing
Blackened scar at the point of nut attachment in fungal cases
treatment: Spray Bordeaux mixture (1%) on bunches at button stage
Ensure adequate irrigation during dry spells to reduce stress shedding
prevention: Avoid moisture stress during flowering and button stage
Balanced boron and potassium nutrition improves fruit retention

pests.name: Rhinoceros Beetle
symptoms: V-shaped cuts on unopened fronds
Boring holes at the crown with chewed fibrous frass
management: Place naphthalene balls or neem cake in the crown leaf axils
Remove and destroy breeding sites (rotting logs, manure pits) nearby
Use pheromone traps for adult beetle control

pests.name: Red Palm Weevil
symptoms: Gum exudation with fibrous chewed matter from trunk holes
Foul fermented odor from the crown region
Crown may topple in advanced infestation
management: Avoid mechanical injuries during pruning that attract egg-laying
Inject systemic insecticide into the trunk on early detection
Install pheromone traps for mass trapping of adults

pests.name: Coconut Eriophyid Mite
symptoms: Triangular yellowish patches near the nut's perianth region
Nuts become malformed with longitudinal streaks
management: Spray neem oil (2%) or wettable sulphur on bunches
Improve overall irrigation to reduce moisture stress

pests.name: Black-Headed Caterpillar
symptoms: Dried, scorched appearance of older leaves
Webbing with fecal matter on the leaf undersurface
management: Release parasitoid Goniozus nephantidis where available
Prune and burn severely infested leaves
Spray neem-based insecticide on active infestations

risk_conditions.impact: Root suffocation, higher bud rot and stem bleeding risk
Button and immature nut shedding, mite infestation risk rises
Leaf scorching and reduced photosynthesis, higher red palm weevil activity
